home.title: Accurate Period Calculator: Private Menstrual Cycle Tracker
home.subtitle: Predict your next cycle, ovulation, and fertile window with our free period calculator. No login required—100% private, secure, and accurate medical-grade tool.

about.title: About Period Calculator
about.subtitle: Empowering women with privacy-first health tools

mission.title: Our Mission
mission.description: We believe every woman deserves access to accurate, private menstrual health tools. Our mission is to provide a reliable period calculator that respects your privacy while helping you understand your body better.

whyWeBuilt.title: Why We Built This
whyWeBuilt.description: Many existing period tracking apps require you to create an account, share personal data with third parties, or worry about data breaches. We wanted to create something different.

items.privacyFirst: Privacy First - All your data stays on your device. We never see or store your personal information.
items.noAccount: No Account Required - Start using immediately without registration or login.
items.accurate: Accurate Predictions - Our algorithms are based on established medical research.
items.free: Completely Free - No premium features, no subscriptions, no hidden costs.

approach.title: Our Approach
approach.description: We are committed to providing accurate, evidence-based information. All our content is:

items.reviewed: Reviewed by professionals - Our content is reviewed by qualified healthcare professionals.
items.sources: Based on authoritative sources - We reference ACOG, NHS, WHO, and other authoritative medical organizations.
items.updated: Regularly updated - We review and update our content quarterly to ensure accuracy.

disclaimer.title: Important Disclaimer
disclaimer.description: Period Calculator is designed for informational purposes only and is not intended as a substitute for professional medical advice, diagnosis, or treatment. Always seek the advice of your physician or other qualified health provider with any questions you may have regarding a medical condition.

contact.title: Get in Touch
contact.description: Have questions or feedback? We'd love to hear from you.

footer.trustText: Your data never leaves your device.

calculator.title: Period Calculator
calculator.subtitle: Track your cycle and predict your next period

privacyPolicy.title: Privacy Policy
privacyPolicy.intro: Your privacy is important to us. This policy explains how we collect, use, and protect your personal information when you use Period Calculator.

dataCollection.title: Data We Collect
dataCollection.description: Period Calculator is designed with privacy as our top priority. When you use our service, we collect minimal data necessary for functionality:
dataCollection.item0: First day of your last period (for cycle calculation)

dataStorage.title: How We Store Your Data
dataStorage.description: All your data is stored locally on your device using browser storage. We never upload your personal data to any server.
dataStorage.item0: Data is stored in your browser's localStorage
dataStorage.item2: You can delete your data at any time by clearing browser cache

dataUsage.title: How We Use Your Data
dataUsage.description: Your data is used exclusively to provide our core services:
dataUsage.item1: Predict your fertile window and ovulation day
dataUsage.item2: Generate calendar events for your predictions

cookies.title: Cookies
cookies.description: We use minimal cookies solely for essential functionality:
cookies.item2: These are functional cookies, not tracking cookies

thirdParty.title: Third-Party Services
thirdParty.description: We integrate with trusted third-party services to enhance your experience:
thirdParty.item0: Google Calendar (for adding events) - data is sent directly from your device to Google
thirdParty.item1: No data is shared with advertising networks

userRights.title: Your Rights
userRights.description: You have complete control over your data:
userRights.item0: Access your data at any time
userRights.item1: Delete your data instantly (clear browser cache)
userRights.item2: Export your data (not applicable as data stays on your device)
userRights.item3: Opt-out by simply not using the app

cta.title: Questions?
cta.description: We're here to help with any privacy concerns.

editorialPolicy.title: Editorial Policy
editorialPolicy.intro: Our editorial policy ensures that all health information provided on Period Calculator is accurate, up-to-date, and based on credible scientific sources. We are committed to transparency in our content creation process.

medicalSources.title: Medical Sources
medicalSources.description: We reference only authoritative medical organizations and peer-reviewed research to ensure our content meets the highest standards of accuracy:
medicalSources.item0: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG)

reviewProcess.title: Content Review Process
reviewProcess.description: Every piece of content goes through a rigorous review process to ensure accuracy and relevance:
reviewProcess.item0: Initial research by qualified health writers
reviewProcess.item2: Regular updates based on latest research

updateFrequency.title: Update Frequency
updateFrequency.description: We maintain our content to reflect the latest medical guidelines and research:
updateFrequency.item1: Medical guidelines updated as new research emerges

accuracyCommitment.title: Accuracy Commitment
accuracyCommitment.description: We strive for precision in all our health information while acknowledging the limitations of predictive tools:
accuracyCommitment.item0: Algorithms based on established medical research

disclaimer.title: Disclaimer
disclaimer.description: Important information about the limitations of our service:
disclaimer.item0: This is a prediction tool, not a medical device
disclaimer.item1: Results are estimates based on averages
disclaimer.item2: Consult healthcare professionals for medical advice
disclaimer.item3: We are not responsible for decisions made based on predictions

step1.title: Enter Your Last Period
step1.description: Input the first day of your most recent period. This is the starting point for all calculations.

step2.title: Set Your Cycle Length
step2.description: Enter your average cycle length (typically 21-35 days). Our algorithm uses this to predict your next period.

step3.title: Add Period Duration
step3.description: Specify how long your period typically lasts (usually 3-7 days). This helps predict the menstruation phase.

step4.title: Get Your Predictions
step4.description: Our algorithm calculates your next period, fertile window, ovulation day, and PMS dates based on medical research.

deepKnowledge.title: Beyond the Dates: Understanding Your Monthly Rhythm
deepKnowledge.description: Most people think of their cycle as just the days they are bleeding, but your body is actually moving through a sophisticated, three-part biological journey every month. Understanding these shifts is exactly why a precise Period Calculator is about more than just 'day one'—it's about mapping your unique hormonal landscape.

follicularPhase.title: The Follicular Phase: The Buildup
follicularPhase.description: The Follicular Phase begins on the very first day of your period and lasts until you ovulate. During this time, your body is busy maturing an egg for release. As your estrogen levels gradually climb, you might notice a steady boost in your energy, mood, and even skin clarity. By tracking the start of this phase in our Period Calculator, the algorithm begins to learn the specific 'tempo' of your body's preparation.

ovulation.title: Ovulation: The Main Event
ovulation.description: Ovulation is the brief but vital window—usually lasting only 12 to 24 hours—when an egg is released from the ovary. This is your peak fertility window. Because factors like stress, travel, or sleep can shift your ovulation date, relying on a simple average isn't always enough. Our tool analyzes your historical data to help you pinpoint this window, whether you are trying to conceive or simply want to stay in tune with your body's signals.

lutealPhase.title: The Luteal Phase: The Countdown
lutealPhase.description: After ovulation, you enter the Luteal Phase. This is the 10-to-16-day stretch where progesterone takes center stage, thickening the uterine lining. If you've ever wondered why you feel more sensitive or bloated a week before your period, this phase is the reason. A consistent Period Calculator helps you predict exactly when this phase begins, so you're never caught off guard by PMS or the start of your next cycle.

latePeriod.question: Am I late for my period?
latePeriod.answer: A period is generally considered "late" if it hasn't arrived within five days of your expected start date. However, since few people have a perfectly rhythmic 28-day cycle, it's normal to see slight fluctuations. If you are sexually active and your period is even one day late, the most reliable next step is a pregnancy test, as our Period Calculator provides an estimate based on averages, not biological certainty.

nextPeriodDate.question: How do I calculate my next period date?
nextPeriodDate.answer: To find your next start date, you first need to identify "Day 1" of your last cycle. This is the very first day you noticed a steady flow. From there, add your average cycle length, which is the number of days from the start of one period to the start of the next. For example, if your last period began on March 1 and your average cycle is 28 days, your next period is predicted to start on March 29.

safePeriod.question: How do I calculate my "safe period"?
safePeriod.answer: The "safe period" refers to the days in your cycle when you are least likely to conceive, typically at the very beginning and very end of your cycle. To calculate this, you must first identify your ovulation window, which is usually 12 to 16 days before your next period. However, it is important to remember that the "Calendar Method" is not 100% accurate. Sperm can survive inside the body for up to five days, making it risky to rely on a calculator alone for contraception.

periodEarly.question: Why did my period come early this month?
periodEarly.answer: It is quite common for a period to arrive a few days early. This usually happens because ovulation occurred sooner than usual. Common triggers for an early cycle include high levels of stress, significant changes in your sleep schedule, intense new exercise routines, or even recent travel. If your period is consistently early or accompanied by unusual pain, consulting a healthcare provider is recommended.

normalFlow.question: How much flow is normal during a period?
normalFlow.answer: The average person loses between 30 ml and 80 ml of blood over the course of their entire period, about 2 to 5 tablespoons. In practical terms, this means soaking through a pad or tampon every 3 to 4 hours. If you find yourself needing to change your protection every hour or noticing clots larger than a quarter, your flow may be considered heavy, and you should seek medical advice.

pregnancyFromLmp.question: Why is pregnancy calculated from the last period (LMP)?
pregnancyFromLmp.answer: Because most people don't know the exact moment of conception, medical professionals use the Last Menstrual Period (LMP) as a clear, objective starting point. Since ovulation usually happens about two weeks after your period starts, this means that during the first two weeks of a "40-week pregnancy," you are not actually pregnant yet. This standard helps doctors more accurately estimate your due date and track fetal development milestones.
